standard.notes: 'En ville' = in town / into town (no article needed after 'en'). 'Aller en ville' = to go into town. 'Une grande ville' = a large city. 'Une petite ville' = a small town. 'La ville de Paris' = the city of Paris. Note: 'la cité' can also mean city but is used in more formal or historical contexts.
examples: Je vais en ville cet après-midi.
I am going into town this afternoon.
Paris est une grande ville magnifique.
Paris is a beautiful large city.

standard.notes: 'Faire les magasins' = to go shopping (literally 'to do the shops'). 'Un grand magasin' = a department store (e.g. Galeries Lafayette, Le Bon Marché). Not to be confused with 'le magazine' (a periodical). 'Le magasin est ouvert' = the shop is open.
examples: Il y a beaucoup de magasins dans le centre-ville.
There are many shops in the town centre.
Le magasin ferme à vingt heures.
The shop closes at 8 PM.

standard.notes: One of the most iconic features of French towns and villages — many French villages are built around a central church. 'L'église Notre-Dame' = Notre-Dame church. French culture is historically Catholic, so churches ('les églises') are prominent landmarks. 'La cathédrale' = the cathedral (a larger church).
examples: L'église est au centre du village.
The church is in the centre of the village.
Nous visitons l'église ce matin.
We are visiting the church this morning.

standard.notes: French town squares ('les places') traditionally feature a central fountain. 'La fontaine est belle' = the fountain is beautiful. 'Une fontaine à eau' = a drinking water fountain. In Paris, 'les fontaines Wallace' are green cast-iron drinking fountains, donated by philanthropist Richard Wallace in 1872.
examples: Il y a une grande fontaine sur la place.
There is a large fountain in the square.
Les enfants jouent près de la fontaine.
The children are playing near the fountain.
standard.special: The iconic Wallace fountains in Paris are named after British philanthropist Richard Wallace.

standard.notes: 'Le jardin public' = public garden / park. 'Le Jardin du Luxembourg' and 'le Jardin des Tuileries' are famous Parisian public gardens. 'Faire du jardinage' = to do gardening. In French urban contexts, 'le jardin' can refer to both a private garden and a public green space.
examples: Le jardin public est à côté de la mairie.
The public garden is next to the town hall.
Nous nous promenons dans le jardin.
We are walking in the garden.

'La ville de Paris' = the city of Paris. Note: 'la cité' can also mean city but is used in more formal or historical contexts.

I am going into town this afternoon.

Paris is a beautiful large city.

examples: Le jardin public est à côté de la mairie.
The public garden is next to the town hall.
Nous nous promenons dans le jardin.
We are walking in the garden.

advanced.notes: France has the highest museum attendance in the world. 'Le musée du Louvre' is the world's most visited museum. 'Visiter un musée' = to visit a museum. 'Le musée est gratuit le premier dimanche du mois' = many French national museums offer free entry on the first Sunday of the month.
examples: Nous allons au musée demain matin.
We are going to the museum tomorrow morning.
Le musée d'Orsay est magnifique.
The Orsay Museum is magnificent.

advanced.meaning: the town centre / the city centre
advanced.notes: 'Au centre-ville' = in the town centre. 'Direction centre-ville' = towards the town centre (common road sign). French town centres ('centres-villes') typically feature pedestrianised streets, markets, and historic buildings. 'Habiter en centre-ville' = to live in the town centre.
examples: Les meilleurs restaurants sont au centre-ville.
The best restaurants are in the town centre.
J'habite à cinq minutes du centre-ville.
I live five minutes from the town centre.

dialogue: Oui, j'habite à Paris. Et vous, vous habitez en France aussi ?
Non, nous habitons en Angleterre — mais on visite la France chaque été.
Ah, c'est bien ! Il y a beaucoup à voir. À Paris, il y a l'église Notre-Dame.
Oui, et le jardin des Tuileries ! On adore se promener là-bas.
Et en France, on peut visiter tellement de villes magnifiques. Lyon, Bordeaux…
On va à Lyon la semaine prochaine. Qu'est-ce qu'il y a à voir là-bas ?
Il y a un vieux quartier magnifique — et de très bons restaurants, bien sûr !

questions.question: Which Paris landmark do they both mention?
Where are Speakers A going next week?

standard.summary: Two friends from different countries talk about visiting France. Speaker A lives in England but visits France every summer; Speaker B lives in Paris. They discuss Paris landmarks including Notre-Dame church and the Tuileries Garden. Speaker A is heading to Lyon the following week, and Speaker B recommends the old quarter and the restaurants there.

dialogue: Alors, vous venez d'où, vous deux ?
Nous venons du Japon — nous sommes à Paris pour une semaine.
Bienvenue en France ! Vous avez visité le centre-ville ?
Oui, nous avons vu le musée d'Orsay et le jardin des Tuileries. C'est magnifique.
Nous allons au Louvre le matin. L'après-midi, on cherche une église ancienne.
Il y a Sainte-Chapelle — c'est dans le centre-ville, à côté de la fontaine Saint-Michel.
Parfait. Et pour les magasins — on peut trouver des souvenirs au centre-ville aussi ?

questions.question: Where are Speaker B and their companion from?
Which two places have they already visited?
options: The Musée d'Orsay and the Tuileries Garden
The centre-ville shops and a fountain
questions.correctAnswer: The Musée d'Orsay and the Tuileries Garden

questions.question: What are they planning to visit in the afternoon?
Where is Sainte-Chapelle, according to Speaker A?
options: In the town centre, next to the Saint-Michel fountain
questions.correctAnswer: In the town centre, next to the Saint-Michel fountain

advanced.summary: Two Japanese visitors discuss their Paris trip with a local. They have already seen the Musée d'Orsay and the Tuileries Garden. The next day, they plan to visit the Louvre in the morning and an old church in the afternoon. The local recommends Sainte-Chapelle, located in the town centre near the Saint-Michel fountain, and the visitors also ask about souvenir shops in the centre.

standard.title: Prepositions with Cities & Countries (à, en, au, aux)
standard.explanation:
In French, the preposition you use to say you are 'in' or 'going to' a place depends on the gender and number of the place name.

**With cities — always 'à':**
- *Je suis à Paris.* = I am in Paris.
- *Je vais à Londres.* = I am going to London.
- *Nous habitons à Lyon.* = We live in Lyon.

**With countries and regions:**

| Type | Preposition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Feminine country (most countries ending in -e) | **en** | *en France, en Espagne, en Angleterre* |
| Masculine country | **au** | *au Japon, au Canada, au Maroc* |
| Plural countries | **aux** | *aux États-Unis, aux Pays-Bas* |
| Countries starting with a vowel | **en** | *en Iran, en Israël* |

**Quick tip for gender:**
Most countries ending in -e are feminine: France (f), Italie (f), Allemagne (f), Espagne (f).
Exceptions: le Mexique (m), le Cambodge (m), le Zimbabwe (m).

**Examples:**
- *Elle vit en France.* = She lives in France.
- *Il vient du Japon.* = He comes from Japan. ('du' = de + au)
- *Nous allons aux États-Unis.* = We are going to the United States.
- *Je reviens de Paris.* = I'm coming back from Paris.

examples.english: They come from the United States.

drills.question: Which preposition goes with 'France' (feminine country)?
drills.explanation: Feminine countries use 'en'. France is feminine, so: en France.

drills.question: Complete: Je vais ___ Paris. (I am going to Paris.)
drills.explanation: Cities always use 'à': à Paris, à Lyon, à Rome.

drills.question: How do you say 'He lives in Japan'?
drills.explanation: 'Le Japon' is masculine, so use 'au' (à + le): au Japon.

drills.question: Complete: Ils habitent ___ États-Unis. (They live in the United States.)
drills.explanation: Plural country names use 'aux' (à + les): aux États-Unis.

drills.question: Which sentence is correct for 'We are going to Spain'?
drills.explanation: 'Espagne' (Spain) is feminine (ends in -e), so use 'en': en Espagne.

advanced.title: Prepositions with Cities & Countries — à, en, au, aux + Exceptions
advanced.explanation:
**Core rules:**

| Place type | Preposition | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| All cities | **à** | à Paris, à Londres, à New York |
| Feminine countries (end in -e) | **en** | en France, en Italie, en Espagne |
| Masculine countries | **au** | au Japon, au Canada, au Portugal |
| Plural countries | **aux** | aux États-Unis, aux Pays-Bas |
| Countries starting with vowel | **en** | en Iran, en Israël, en Ouganda |
| French regions (most feminine) | **en** | en Bretagne, en Normandie, en Provence |
| French regions (masculine) | **dans le / au** | dans le Midi, au Périgord |

**'Coming from' — de, du, des:**

| From | Preposition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| City | de / d' | Je viens de Paris. / Je viens d'Oslo. |
| Feminine country | de / d' | Je viens de France. / d'Italie. |
| Masculine country | du | Je viens du Japon. |
| Plural country | des | Je viens des États-Unis. |

**Key exceptions to memorise:**
- **le Mexique** (m) → *au Mexique* (not 'en' despite -e ending)
- **le Cambodge** (m) → *au Cambodge*
- **le Mozambique** (m) → *au Mozambique*

**Islands — special rule:**
Small / close islands often use 'à': *à Cuba, à Malte, à Chypre*
Larger / distant islands use 'en': *en Corse, en Sardaigne*

**Prepositions with continents:**
- *en Europe, en Afrique, en Asie, en Amérique du Nord/Sud, en Océanie*
- *dans* is occasionally used with continents in formal contexts.

examples.french: Nous allons en Italie, puis au Maroc.
examples.english: We are going to Italy, then to Morocco.

examples.french: Elle vient du Canada mais habite en France.
examples.english: She comes from Canada but lives in France.

examples.french: Ils sont aux États-Unis pour le travail.
examples.english: They are in the United States for work.

examples.french: Je viens d'Oslo — je suis en visite à Paris.
examples.english: I come from Oslo — I am visiting Paris.

drills.question: How do you say 'She lives in Mexico'?
drills.explanation: 'Le Mexique' is masculine (exception — ends in -e but is masculine). Use 'au': au Mexique.

drills.question: Complete: Je viens ___ Japon. (I come from Japan.)
drills.explanation: 'Du' = de + le. For masculine countries: je viens du Japon.

drills.question: Which is correct for 'We are going to Corsica'?
drills.explanation: Corse (Corsica) is a larger island — it uses 'en': en Corse. Small islands use 'à'.

drills.question: Complete: Elle revient ___ États-Unis. (She's coming back from the United States.)
drills.explanation: 'Des' = de + les. For plural countries: elle revient des États-Unis.

drills.question: How do you say 'They work in Asia'?
drills.explanation: Continents always use 'en': en Europe, en Asie, en Afrique, en Amérique.

drills.question: Complete: Nous allons ___ Bretagne cet été. (We are going to Brittany this summer.)
drills.explanation: French regions ending in -e are typically feminine: en Bretagne, en Normandie, en Provence.

drills.question: Which sentence is correct?
drills.explanation: 'Du' = de + le. Canada is masculine: il vient du Canada.

drills.question: Complete: Je visite ___ musées ___ centre-ville de Paris. (I'm visiting the museums in the town centre of Paris.)
drills.explanation: 'Les musées' = the museums (plural definite article). The full phrase: je visite les musées du centre-ville de Paris.

standard.question: Which preposition is used for feminine countries?
standard.explanation: Feminine countries (most ending in -e) use 'en': en France, en Espagne, en Italie.

standard.question: What does 'le jardin public' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Le jardin public' = the public garden or park. Famous examples include the Jardin du Luxembourg in Paris.

standard.question: Complete: Je vais ___ Japon. (I am going to Japan.)
standard.explanation: 'Le Japon' is masculine, so use 'au' (à + le): au Japon.

standard.question: What does 'un grand magasin' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Un grand magasin' = a department store (like Galeries Lafayette). Not a magazine — 'magazine' is written the same but means a periodical.

standard.question: How do you say 'We are going to the United States'?
standard.explanation: 'Les États-Unis' is plural, so use 'aux' (à + les): aux États-Unis.

advanced.question: How do you say 'She comes from Mexico'?
advanced.explanation: 'Le Mexique' is masculine (exception — ends in -e). 'Du' = de + le: elle vient du Mexique.

advanced.question: Complete: Ils habitent ___ Pays-Bas. (They live in the Netherlands.)
What is 'le musée d'Orsay' famous for?
advanced.explanation: The Musée d'Orsay in Paris is famous for its Impressionist and post-Impressionist collections, including works by Monet, Renoir, and Van Gogh.

advanced.question: How do you say 'I am going to Corsica'?
advanced.explanation: Corse (Corsica) is a larger island — it uses 'en': en Corse. Smaller islands use 'à'.

advanced.question: Complete: Il revient ___ Canada. (He is coming back from Canada.)
advanced.explanation: 'Du' = de + le. Canada is masculine: il revient du Canada.

advanced.question: Which is correct for 'We are going to Normandy'?
advanced.explanation: French regions ending in -e are typically feminine: en Normandie, en Bretagne, en Provence.

standard.scenario: Describe your city or neighbourhood to a French pen pal. Mention what there is to see and where things are located. Use 'à', 'en', 'au' / 'aux' to say where different things are.
keyPhrases: Dans ma ville, il y a… (In my town, there is…)
L'église / le jardin / la fontaine est à… (The church / garden / fountain is in…)
Comparé à Paris / en France… (Compared to Paris / in France…)
standard.modelAnswer: Bonjour ! Je viens d'Angleterre — j'habite dans une ville au bord de la mer. Dans ma ville, il y a une vieille église et un grand jardin public. Il n'y a pas de fontaine célèbre, mais il y a beaucoup de magasins. En France, j'adore les fontaines et les vieux quartiers. Je veux visiter Paris — je vais à Paris l'année prochaine !
standard.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses 'je viens de' for origin, describes the city with 'il y a', makes a comparison with France using 'en France', and ends with a future plan using 'aller à + city'.
standard.tip: Remember the key rule: cities always use 'à', feminine countries use 'en', masculine countries use 'au'.

advanced.scenario: Describe your city to a French pen pal. Mention where things are using all preposition rules (à, en, au, aux), compare your city with a French city, and use 'venir de' to talk about origin and recent actions.
keyPhrases: Je viens de… / Je viens d'arriver de… (I come from… / I have just arrived from…)
En France, on peut… / À Paris, il y a… (In France, you can… / In Paris, there is…)
Le musée / le centre-ville est… (The museum / town centre is…)
Comparé au centre-ville de Paris… (Compared to the town centre of Paris…)
Je reviens des États-Unis / du Japon / d'Espagne. (I'm back from the US / Japan / Spain.)
advanced.modelAnswer: Bonjour ! Je viens d'Angleterre, d'une ville au bord de la mer. Dans mon centre-ville, il y a un vieux musée et un jardin public avec une belle fontaine. Il y a aussi beaucoup de magasins et une église du douzième siècle. Comparé à Paris, ma ville est petite — mais il y a une bonne ambiance. En France, j'adore aller en Bretagne — la côte est magnifique. L'année prochaine, je vais en Espagne et puis au Maroc.
advanced.modelAnswerExplanation: The answer uses 'je viens de' for origin, describes the town centre with key vocab, makes a comparison with Paris using 'à Paris', uses 'en Bretagne' for a French region, and correctly deploys 'en Espagne' and 'au Maroc' for the travel plans.
